course_description:
Financial statement analysis, traditional procedures and current developments. Use of accounting data for valuation of firms.

This course is designed to expose you to the 'state of the art' approach to valuing the shares of a company. Valuation is a highly speculative undertaking. As such, this course introduces a systematic approach to valuation that reduces the input of pure speculation by emphasising the importance of knowing the business, the business environment, and the appropriate data and valuation model to use for the type of company you are trying to value. Some students are impatient with the upfront, fundamental 'business and accounting' analysis requirements, but beware the valuation failures of the financial analyst who does not thoroughly understand the company's fundamentals.

Students who routinely do not attend class may experience difficulties in this course.

Course Changes in Response to Previous Student Feedback

A group project has been introduced in this course. The project requires students to complete a detailed financial statement analysis of an Australian-listed company, write a valuation report, and present the analysis.

course_aims: This course aims to provide an introduction to business analysis, financial statement analysis and valuation using financial statement data. It introduces a systematic, state of the art approach to valuation that reduces the input of pure speculation by emphasising the importance of knowing the business, the business environment, and the appropriate data and valuation model.

learning_outcomes.description: Prepare reformulated financial statements to facilitate financial statement analysis and equity valuation.
Undertake financial ratio analysis to determine the drivers of profitability and growth.
Assess accounting quality and familiarise with the motivations for earnings management.
Prepare pro forma forecasts of the financial statements based on sales forecasts, growth rates, and ratios.

Work collaboratively in a team to develop and present a cohesive financial statement analysis and valuation report by using the appropriate valuation model and employing clear communication to support insights and final buy-sell recommendations.

assessment_details.title: Financial Statement Analysis Project - Part 1
assessment_details.other_conditions: Peer assessment factor. See the conditions definitions
assessment_details.task_description: This project requires students to complete a detailed financial statement analysis of an Australian-listed company. The project will be completed in three parts. Part one of the project is based on the concepts taught in the first four classes . Note also: Detailed instructions will be provided on Blackboard. Students need to choose a company to analyse . This is a group assessment. You must provide your supporting workings in Excel. Excel work must reflect correct financial modelling conventions, which will be discussed in the class. AI Statement: Artificial Intelligence (AI) and Machine Translation (MT) are emerging tools that may support students in completing this assessment task. Students may appropriately use AI and/or MT in completing this assessment task. Students must clearly reference any use of AI or MT in each instance. A failure to reference generative AI or MT use may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct. Read full task description Hide full task description
assessment_details.submission_guidelines:
Please submit your Excel workings through Blackboard.
Deferral or extension
You may be able to apply for an extension .
The maximum extension allowed is 14 days. Extensions are given in multiples of 24 hours.
Late submission
A penalty of 10% of the maximum possible mark will be deducted per 24 hours from time submission is due for up to 7 days. After 7 days, you will receive a mark of 0.

Part three of the project requires the students to present their analysis completed in part two. Each group will be given a maximum of ten minutes to present their valuation report and recommendation. Note also: Detailed instructions will be provided on Blackboard. This is a group assessment. learning_activities.topic: Lecture 1 - Introduction Course introduction, motivation and accounting recap
Workshop 1 - Introduction
Lecture 2 - Accounting Reformulation I Reformulation of the Balance Sheet
Workshop 2 - Accounting Reformulation I
Lecture 3 - Accounting Reformulation II Reformulation of the Income Statement/Statement of Comprehensive Income
Workshop 3 - Accounting Reformulation II
Lecture 4 - Accounting Reformulation III Reformulation of the Cash Flow statement and reformulated financial statement relations
Workshop 4 - Accounting Reformulation III
Lecture 5 - Ratio Analysis DuPont analysis of financial ratios
Workshop 5 - Ratio Analysis
Lecture 6 - Valuation & Forecasting I Introduction to fundamental valuation and levered valuation models
In-Semester Break
Workshop 6 - Valuation & Forecasting I
Lecture 7 - Valuation & Forecasting II Unlevered valuation models - DCF and Residual Operating Income
Workshop 7 - Valuation & Forecasting II
Lecture 8 - Valuation & Forecasting III Forecasting the financial statements and valuation based on our own forecasts
Workshop 8 - Valuation & Forecasting III
Lecture 9 - Accounting Quality I Introduction to earnings management, manipulation of accruals and fair values

Workshop 9 - Accounting Quality I Labour Day Public Holiday - Monday 4th May 2026 - Check Blackboard for announcements about affected classes. - Check Blackboard for announcements about affected classes.
Lecture 10 - Accounting Quality II Manipulation of revenue/expenses and diagnostic ratios
Workshop 10 - Accounting Quality II
Lecture 11 - Accounting Quality III Earnings management case study
Group Presentations I
Lecture 12 - Advanced Topics Advanced topics in financial statement analysis
Lecture 13 - Summary and Revision Course review and exam information
Group Presentations II
